Title: Last Boohoo Order - Returns Policy
Text: I won’t be ordering from Boohoo nor any concession within its wider Debenhams parent group again, on principle. Having paid £3.99 for delivery, I was sent the incorrect item. However, to return this item cost me a further £1.99 which will eventually be deducted from the refund amount I will receive. The customer should not have to pay an additional (item return) charge for Boohoo’s incompetence in its basic retail activity of selecting and sending the correct product that was ordered. While £1.99 appears miniscule, it nonetheless represents a 50% increase in delivery charges. If this occurs on a regular basis across the entire Debenhams retail group, however, it is a great money making ruse where the customer ends up with nothing but the company inflates income made from delivery charges in reward for its own potentially deliberate error.

Title: Used Items Resold as New
Text: I had my eye on a Ralph Lauren jumper for a while and couldn't find it anywhere else. It finally came back into stock during a sale, so I ordered it straight away. When it arrived, it was covered in pet hair, smelled strongly of aftershave, had no tags attached, had visible marks, and several pulled threads. It clearly did not appear to be in the condition I would expect for a new item. I spent over an hour speaking with customer services and explained that I would be happy to keep the jumper if a partial refund could be offered to reflect its condition, especially as it is now sold out and cannot be replaced. However, I was told the only option was to return it for inspection, and a refund would only be considered if it was deemed to have a manufacturing fault. The most frustrating part is that if I return it and it is simply put back into stock, another customer could end up receiving the same item. Very disappointing experience and not what I would expect when purchasing a premium brand item. -------------------- **UPDATE:** I returned the item, and when it came back into stock for £80 more, I bought it again. To my disbelief, I have been sent the exact same faulty item. ASOS had clearly brushed the item in an attempt to remove the original mark, but the stitching across the back was still missing. I was specifically told to return it as faulty, and I even included a note clearly highlighting the defect to make sure it was not sent to another customer—especially as someone could easily wear it before noticing the missing stitching and then find themselves unable to return it. After paying £80 more, waiting over a week for the order to be dispatched, and expecting a replacement, I have now received the exact same defective item back. Absolutely appalling.

Title: ASOS used to be my go-to online…
Text: ASOS used to be my go-to online retailer for trendy clothing and accessories, but it has gone downhill. Delivery is no longer truly “express —even Premier members often wait two or more days for their orders. The quality of the clothing has also noticeably declined. My biggest issue is ASOS’s Fair Trade Policy. Because ASOS has no physical stores, customers have to order clothing to try it on. Yet ASOS monitors how much you return and can restrict or even close your account based on your return rate, without giving you a meaningful opportunity to explain yourself. That’s simply not fair when many returns are due to sizing or fit—something you can’t know until you’ve received and tried on the items. A fair policy should recognize the reality of online shopping, not penalize loyal customers for ordering multiple sizes or styles to find the right fit. Between the slower shipping, declining quality, and this restrictive Fair Trade Policy, I’ve found other online retailers now offer a much better customer experience.

Title: Loyal customer for years, then my account was closed
Text: I have been an ASOS customer for many years and estimate I’ve spent well over £30,000 with them over that time. The overwhelming majority of my orders arrived without any issues. I reported the missing items in good faith, and the very next day I received an email saying my refund had been refused and my account had been permanently closed. I was accused of breaching their fair use policy with no meaningful explanation or evidence provided. After years of loyalty and tens of thousands of pounds spent, I found it shocking that reporting genuinely missing items resulted in me losing both my account and the refund I believe I was entitled to. To make matters worse, I had already created returns for several recent orders before my account was closed. Thankfully, customer service confirmed those returns would still be honoured, but the whole experience has been incredibly stressful and disappointing. Loyal customers deserve better treatment than this. Based on my experience, I would think twice before placing expensive orders with ASOS

Title: How a Damaged Parcel Turned Into a…
Text: How a Damaged Parcel Turned Into a Permanently Closed Account I hadn’t ordered from ASOS for several years before placing this order. When my parcel arrived, it had a hole in the packaging and two makeup items were missing. I contacted ASOS immediately, sent photographs of the damaged parcel, and followed the instructions I was given. ASOS confirmed that my order should have contained four items. After I confirmed that only two had arrived, they approved a refund for the missing products and told me it would be paid within 10 working days. I thought the matter had been resolved. A few days later, I received an email saying that my refund had been refused, my account had been permanently closed, and that my account activity was not in line with ASOS’s Fair Use Policy. This came as a complete surprise. I had simply reported a damaged parcel and followed ASOS’s own process. When I tried to understand what had happened, I discovered that there was no real opportunity to discuss the decision with anyone. Instead, I was informed that any further emails about the matter would be automatically closed without a response, leaving me with no way of asking questions or explaining my case. Wanting to understand the reason for such a serious decision, I submitted a Data Subject Access Request. The documents ASOS provided confirmed exactly what I had told them from the beginning: that I had reported the damaged parcel, provided photographs, and that their own system had approved the refund. Despite this, I was never given any explanation as to why the refund was reversed or what evidence led ASOS to conclude that my account activity was not in line with their Fair Use Policy. I was simply told that it was a business decision. The money was never the real issue. What disappointed me was the complete lack of transparency. I followed ASOS’s instructions, relied on the refund approval communicated by their own system, and was ultimately left without either the approved refund or any meaningful explanation as to why that decision had been reversed or why my account had been permanently closed.

Title: Mammoth return charges!!
Text: Boohoo have somehow managed to turn the simple act of returning an item into a masterclass in daylight robbery. You order clothes, they don’t fit, you send them back — fair enough. But then the real performance begins. Delivery charges, return charges, and then mysterious “additional charges” slapped on top like some backstreet accountant making it up as they go along. Over £20 gone. Vanished. And for what exactly? Nobody at Boohoo seems capable of explaining where these extra costs even come from. You ask for clarification and get the corporate equivalent of shrugging shoulders and hiding behind templated emails. It’s like dealing with a bookmaker after a dodgy VAR decision — all confidence when taking your money, complete silence when asked to justify it. The whole thing feels deliberately opaque. They lure people in with cheap prices, then claw the money back through hidden charges and disgraceful customer service. If this is their idea of “customer care,” they may as well just put a sign on the website saying: “Thanks for your money — now good luck getting any of it back.” Absolute shambles of a company. Avoid if you value transparency, fairness, or basic respect as a customer.

The biggest issue is that the poor quality often isn't immediately obvious. It typically becomes apparent only after a couple of weeks of wearing the item, by which point you're already approaching the end of the return window. The industry standard is typically a return window of 14–30 days to return an item once the return window opens. Save your money and shop elsewhere. Quality is awful for what you pay for. Title: Very disappointed with my experience
Text: Very disappointed with my experience. My order was given an estimated delivery date of 20 June 2026, yet on the day of delivery I was informed that the order was still being processed and had not even been dispatched. It is frustrating to provide customers with a delivery estimate when the item has not left the warehouse. Communication has been poor, and I have been left without a clear explanation for the delay. If there are stock or processing issues, customers should be informed promptly rather than having to chase for updates. I understand that delays can happen, but transparency and accurate delivery information are essential. Unfortunately, this experience has left me with little confidence in the service. This has been one of the most frustrating online shopping experiences I have had. I placed this order because I needed it for an important event the following day. I relied on the estimated delivery date provided by the company and planned accordingly. Imagine my frustration when, on the supposed delivery date, I was informed that my order had not even been dispatched and was still being processed. It is completely unacceptable to advertise delivery estimates that the company has no realistic prospect of meeting. If an order has not left the warehouse, customers should not be led to believe it will arrive on a specific date. What made matters worse was the response from customer service. After the inconvenience, disappointment and stress caused by this delay, the manager appeared to think that refunding the express shipping fee would somehow resolve the issue. The issue was never the delivery charge. The issue was that the item did not arrive when promised and was therefore useless for the event for which it was purchased. Communication throughout has been poor. I received no proactive update regarding the delay and was left chasing for information myself. Had I been informed earlier, I could have made alternative arrangements. Instead, I was left scrambling at the last minute because the company failed to provide accurate information about my order. Delays happen, but misleading delivery expectations, poor communication, and a lack of accountability are much harder to excuse. This experience has completely undermined my confidence in the company, and I will be very reluctant to place another order in the future.

Title: Warranty claim denied despite legal obligation – avoid customer service
Text: I purchased Adidas Originals Firebird Shorts from ASOS in July 2025. After less than 11 months, the seams started coming apart. I reported this on 3 June 2026. What followed was one of the worst customer service experiences I have had with any retailer: •	The chat assistant confirmed a replacement would be arranged and that a human agent would follow up within 24 hours. I waited 5 days. •	I was then contacted by four different agents across multiple emails, none of whom had read the prior conversation. •	I was asked to resubmit photos I had already sent via chat. •	Every single response rejected my claim as “wear and tear” – with zero legal or factual basis provided. Under German consumer law (§ 477 BGB), any defect appearing within the first 12 months of purchase is legally presumed to be a manufacturing fault. The burden of proof lies with the seller, not the customer. ASOS never addressed this argument once across multiple emails. I escalated formally and set a 7-day deadline. The response was another copy-paste rejection from yet another agent who clearly had not read my email. I have now filed a complaint with the Verbraucherzentrale Berlin and the EU ODR platform. I will not be using ASOS again.

Title: Poor Handling of a Manufacturing Defect
Text: I attempted to make a claim with ASOS for an On product that developed what I believe to be a manufacturing defect. Under the Consumer Rights Act, consumers have statutory rights regarding faulty goods that extend beyond a retailer’s standard returns policy. Despite this, ASOS has failed to properly address my claim or provide a satisfactory resolution. I have spent far more time chasing this issue than any customer should reasonably be expected to, and there appears to be little effort from ASOS to resolve the matter. Title: 'Ethical' fast fashion?
Text: Rapanui make attractive looking clothes, with a casual, surfy, nature-themed look. Unfortunately they refuse to publish slightly critical product reviews on their website, creating a false appearance of entirely happy customers. For instance my fair, truthful, 3* review of a jacket with a badly designed hood has not been published, and I'm unable to re-submit any review for this product as they tell me I've already submitted my review. It's apparently an ethical brand, and around 10 years ago when I was first started wearing their clothes, there was a big emphasis on their relationship with an Indian textile factory committed to fair labour practices.  However, more products are listed as being made in China (not known for particularly good labour laws & human rights) with no further explanation of their labour sourcing, leading me to assume their focus is more on the materials (i.e. organic cotton) and more environmentally friendly processes. Where Rapanui let down is on durability.  I have over time had to throw a lot of boxers away, & send a large number of t-shirts to be re-milled. Their circular fashion concept is their way of keeping the prices fairly low, & it looks appealing on paper that you'll get a coupon for returning, but it's only cost effective if you're already buying over £50 worth of goods, otherwise the £5 off voucher (you can only use one at a time)  is absorbed by the nearly-equivalent delivery fee. And often, I've had trouble with the codes, and it has taken a while for customer service to get back to me, but to be fair they have always resolved it. On balance I still like some of what they do, and buy the odd item, but less wedded to the brand these days. Because most of their t shirts are a lighter weight 155 g/m2 I've been looking for higher thread-count items but these are hard to come by. Even though I question how sustainable they are, there's not many or any competitors on the same level with a similar offering, so I intend to keep Rapanui on my radar for clothes shopping, for the meanwhile.

Title: ASOS Ignored Evidence, Refused Assistance, and Left Me Without My £180 Refund
Text: ASOS refused to take responsibility for a £180 return even after InPost confirmed a system issue I have been a regular ASOS customer, but the way ASOS handled my return issue has been completely unacceptable. I placed my ASOS return parcel into an InPost locker on 18 April 2026 at BEDWELL CRES, Stevenage, SG1 1NA. The parcel contained items worth approximately £180. After placing the parcel in the locker, I never received a collection confirmation or proper tracking update. I contacted InPost and was told that there was an issue with the locker/system. InPost also stated in writing that there were issues with my parcel entering their systems correctly and specifically told me to report the problem to ASOS so ASOS could raise it with InPost on my behalf and work together to resolve it. I provided this information to ASOS. I explained repeatedly that the parcel was physically stuck inside the locker, that the locker was not working, that I had no access to the parcel, and that InPost had directed me back to ASOS. I also provided the locker location, the date of drop-off, the return details, and a photo of the locker. Despite all of this, ASOS refused to take ownership of the issue. Their response was essentially that they could not “just take my word without proof” and that I needed InPost to confirm the drop-off. But I had already shown them InPost’s own message confirming there were system issues and asking ASOS to raise the matter with them. ASOS then told me that the issue was “not handled by ASOS” and that I needed to deal directly with the carrier. This is exactly the problem: InPost kept sending me back to ASOS, and ASOS kept sending me back to InPost. I was trapped between two companies, while my return parcel remained inaccessible and my £180 refund was withheld. This is not acceptable customer service. ASOS generated the return, ASOS instructed the return process, and InPost specifically told me ASOS needed to raise the issue with them. Instead of helping, ASOS dismissed the situation and left me to chase the carrier myself. I have been waiting since 18 April with no refund, no clear resolution, and no proper support. Text: I am extremely disappointed with my recent experience with ASOS, which managed to go wrong at every stage of the process. For my birthday, I purchased a pair of shoes originally priced at £140. The returned shoes arrived at the warehouse yesterday, but I received no update regarding the exchange. This made me suspicious, so I contacted customer service on 11 June. The shoes arrived in a severely damaged box. 2. ASOS’s system failed to process my exchange request despite confirming it. 3. The replacement discount code provided by customer service does not work. The customer service was completely unhelpful throughout the entire process, and no reasonable solution was offered. Items frequently appear to be available, only to disappear and show as unavailable when selected. My biggest disappointment is not the removal of Next Day Delivery itself, but the way ASOS has handled communication, refunds and customer support. Customers should not have to spend hours chasing refunds for services that are no longer being provided.

Title: a return to clothing as a form of respect
Text: 'There are garments one wears… and then there are garments one enters into relationship with. My experience with Rapanui has been of the latter. I came to them quietly, seeking not simply comfort, but congruence — a harmony between what touches my skin and what I hold to be true. Their pieces, particularly the underwear and trackies I now find myself living in, carry a rare integrity. The fabric does not feel manufactured in haste; it feels considered, almost reverent. There is a softness that is not merely tactile, but energetic — as though the material itself has been allowed to remain close to its origin. Rapanui’s commitment to organic fibres, circular design, and renewable production is not just branding rhetoric; it is perceptible. One senses it in the breathability of the cotton, in the absence of harshness, in the way the garments settle on the body without resistance. It is clothing that does not impose — it accompanies. In a world saturated with synthetic urgency, there is something profoundly grounding about wearing pieces that feel clean in both composition and intention. For those of us who are attentive to the subtle dialogue between body, environment, and energy, this matters deeply. What we wear becomes an extension of our field. I have worn their underwear and trackies not as occasional comforts, but as daily companions — and they have held their shape, their softness, and their quiet presence with grace. Others speak similarly of their quality and natural materials, often highlighting their durability and comfort, alongside a genuine commitment to sustainability. This is not fast fashion. It is something slower, more conscious — a return, perhaps, to clothing as a form of respect: for the body, for the earth, and for the unseen threads that connect the two. For me, purity in clothing is not an aesthetic preference; it is a practice. And in that regard, Rapanui feels aligned.'

Title: Buyer beware!
Text: I ordered a white lace dress with bell sleeves from Missguided. When the dress arrived and I tried it on, the sleeves were huge and overhung past my fingertips. I started the returns process which was a nightmare. I was constantly having to email them then wait weeks for a reply. Eventually, I had to take photographs with a tape measure on the garment to show the problem,  which I did, then wait weeks again for a reply. Eventually, they said to return the garment but they wouldn't reimburse me for the cost of the return postage. I had to chase them up for weeks again regarding my refund, I then received an email asking for proof of postage. Luckily,  I'd screenshot the tracking emails from Royal Mail which showed the items journey and even a photo of it at their depot. Still no response from them so I had to email again asking for my refund. A few days later, I received a reply saying I'd get a $19.99 refund, yet I'd paid $5.22 postage for the item to be sent and nearly £4 for it to be sent back to them. It's outrageous when the item they sent is manufactured incorrectly, with the sleeves being 4 inches longer than a normal sized ladies sleeve. How are women expected to wear this dress when the sleeves overhang past their fingertips by 4 inches? So buyer beware, the item you receive may be totally the wrong size, looks nothing like the photograph or is flawed but you'll have to pay the postage both ways if you want to return it. A Chinese company masquerading as a British company with no qualms about sending you inferior, missized ladies clothing.

I had the best couple of hours; I felt confident, creative, and genuinely happy — something I really needed after a tough week. This experience has given me the confidence to personalise my Lucy & Yak dungarees and aprons with my own designs, and I couldn’t be more delighted with what I created. See photos attached. Please could you pass this message on to head office and to Joe, and let him know how much his kindness and encouragement meant to me? Title: “Don’t Charge Us to Advertise for You!”
Text: I am outraged by Primark’s decision to start charging 15p for paper bags. This isn’t about sustainability; it’s an obvious money grab at the expense of customers. These bags are flimsy, tear easily, and have your branding plastered all over them—essentially, we’re being asked to pay for the privilege of being walking billboards for your company. This charge feels completely unjustified. Customers should never be forced to pay for something so low-quality and disposable. If you truly cared about sustainability, you would offer durable, reusable bags, not flimsy ones that break after a single use. Charging customers for these bags preys on our trust, and it’s clear that this is just another way to increase profits under the guise of “helping the environment.” What’s even worse is that this unfair practice is now becoming common across retailers. But I refuse to accept this as the new normal. As customers, we need to stand up and reject any company that tries to profit off us by charging for bags that have no real value. Primark should be ashamed of this decision, and I will no longer shop at a store that disrespects its customers this way. I strongly encourage everyone to stop supporting this exploitative practice. Don’t let Primark—or any other retailer—think they can get away with charging us for something that should be part of our shopping experience without an extra cost. Refuse to accept it. Speak up. Demand better.

Title: Disappointing Checkout Experience at Primark Llanelli
Text: Visited the Llanelli Primark store recently to pick up some clothing. The shopping experience started off well—the range of clothes was reasonable, and the changing rooms were spacious and easy to access. However, things took a sharp downturn at the checkout. The entire checkout process was poorly designed and frustrating. The area appears to have been recently revamped, but not for the better. There was only one staff member handling sales and returns at a very small counter, and another stationed at a separate area where customers were funneled into a queue to use self-service checkouts. The self-checkouts themselves were confusing and inconsistent—some had handheld scanners, others did not. Trying to scan clothes, pay extra for a bag, and locate a printed receipt turned into a chaotic task. To make matters worse, as we attempted to exit, we were abruptly stopped and spoken to rudely by a staff member. Apparently, you’re required to scan your receipt in order to leave the area—something that was never made clear during the process. It left a sour taste after what had been a decent in-store experience. A little more signage, clearer instructions, and better staffing could make a world of difference. Until then, be prepared for a stressful finish to your shopping trip.

Title: Poor Service, Awful In-store Experience - Why bother when there is better
Text: I was told if I was to contact customer services that you would be able to help. This week I tried to return some items that I bought before my holidays and I was refused the return or exchange for items I had taken to the till due to them being slightly outside the 28 return date. I explained to the store attendant and then supervisor that I am 28 weeks pregnant and have been poorly and unable to return these items. The items I wanted to return total between £50- 60 which is a lot to lose with items that don't fit me and a few that didn't fit my children. As you are aware there is a bit of a culture with Primark that encourages us to spend freely with lower prices, however I am so disappointed that I have done that and been met with such a strict returns policy. As a bit of insight I would like to say I was 25 weeks pregnant when I bought these items, your changing rooms on the women's wear level were closed, the store was extremely hot, staff directed me to other changing rooms that were just as busy and then then I queued for about 30 minutes to make my purchase. Its really hard and a challenge to shop there when you are not pregnant never mind pregnant. At the very least I was only expecting to exchange - no more but I travelled all the way to Warrington to have a wasted journey and it just really upset me as other retailers (like New Look) didn't even ask they just did the returns without a query. I was even more disappointed that the human factor didn't exist to understand the challenges as a pregnant woman in your store has had and why I just bought items without trying on. With other retailers relaxing their returns policy, some going to unlimited! It’s clear that it encourages repeat purchases that are far more valuable. PRIMARK VALUE NO ONE just want you in and out of their stores and your money. Zero service. Get to a better shop, plenty that have better quality online and in stores like new look that deliver to your door and don’t have this issue. Title: Non-colourfast dyes; they run, they stain!
Text: Decent stores, nice staff (where I've visted). The clothes are unsurprisingly very bad and not for the reason you might first expect. The problem is the dyes. The stitching of clothes is usually great and the materials are fairly good, but none of the garments have good quality fast dyes. End result? After a few washes your garment fades, or worse; if it is black, navy or dark grey it will end up with brownish red stains where the red dye has run and the others haven't. This actually makes Primark terrible value for money as you'd need to replace every garment that does this far more often than if you'd paid double the price elsewhere. Think, one pair of shorts from Primark lasting a month or two versus a well made pair with good dyes lasting one or two years, perhaps longer. I won't shop here again. I don't shop here exclusively, but this is one of the few stores I keep having to buy replacements for. Matalan was the same. Cheap dyes ruin otherwise good clothing. Bad value. Bad quality non-colourfast dyes. There really is no point stepping foot in this shop. Note: all washed per the label unstructions (40°C) & washed with similar colours, inside out.

Title: My review is about return policy
Text: My review is about return policy. There are few, if any, stores in NY that only have a 28 day return policy. At the very least the return window is 30 days. Being used to that policy, I went to Primark to return something at the 30 day mark....two days late. The cashier told me she could not help me; that the policy is a strict 28 days. I could not get my money back for the items that fit no one, nor could I even exchange them for some other merchandise. After I returned home, I called the store manager and spoke to the one on duty at that time. She said they do make exceptions for a day or two, so she told me I could come back the same day and she would help me. I am a senior citizen, had just recently gotten home from the store and really did not feel able to go out again. I told her I could come back in the morning, but she said she would only allow the refund til end of day. So even though I am tired out, I have to get myself back to the store; spend more money to get there and more time. Their return policy is narrower than any other and I will not be shopping for anyone in that store again.
